text: The surgeon opens the ovarian cortex along the anti-mesenteric border, creating a long incision approximately twelve to fifteen centimeters in length.
The video shows bilateral ovarian dermoids, each measuring approximately twelve to fifteen centimeters, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in separating the tissue planes due to the prolonged presence of the large dermoid cysts within the ovary.
The surgical field reveals a vascular plane, likely due to the prolonged presence of the dermoid cyst, with visible blood vessels within the tissue.
The surgeon begins stripping the ovarian cortex, revealing a weak spot in the tissue that is visually identifiable.
The surgeon introduces an endobag into the surgical field, preparing to contain and remove the ovarian dermoids.
Within the peritoneal cavity, the surgeon places the entire ovarian cyst along with the ovarian cortex inside the endobag to continue the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon empties part of the ovarian cyst to debulk it before proceeding with the cystectomy, reducing its size within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the cystectomy inside an endobag, carefully manipulating the ovarian cyst to prevent any spillage of its contents.

The surgeon utilizes an endobag to contain the ovarian cyst and surrounding ovarian cortex during the cystectomy, a crucial step to prevent spillage, especially in cases of stage one ovarian cancer where an oophorectomy is performed.
The surgeon addresses the opposite side of the ovarian cyst using the same technique as previously demonstrated, ensuring careful manipulation to prevent spillage.
The surgeon makes a large incision and performs the cystectomy, removing the ovarian cyst from the surgical field.
The surgeon places the visible dermoid cyst inside the endobag, ensuring containment of the cyst contents during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a cystectomy with careful dissection to avoid entering the wrong plane, which could result in significant bleeding.

The surgeon removes the entire endobag from the abdominal cavity and flushes the peritoneal cavity with approximately five to six liters of normal saline, ensuring thorough irrigation before completing the procedure.
